title: The End Of Boom/Bust Cycles For The Memory Market
gist: The author argues the GenAI boom has structurally ended the memory industry's decades-long boom/bust cycle, letting the ~3.25 DRAM makers and 4 flash makers price on unrelenting demand rather than commodity cyclicality — evidenced by Micron's Q3 F2026 revenue hitting $41.46 billion (up 4.5X year-on-year) and its HBM TAM forecast for 2026-2030 roughly doubling to $724 billion in 18 months, a dynamic the author says persists only as long as the GenAI bubble does not burst.

themes.theme: Structural end of the memory boom/bust cycle

insights.claim: The author's central thesis is that the GenAI boom has, for the first time in five decades, let memory makers (Samsung, SK Hynix, Micron, ChangXin Memory Technologies for DRAM; plus Kioxia/Western Digital for flash) consistently and predictably make money without the traditional boom/bust pricing cycle.
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: If true, memory makers deserve a valuation re-rating away from cyclical-commodity multiples toward more durable, demand-locked pricing power multiples, as long as GenAI demand persists — a key thesis for anyone holding or evaluating Micron, SK Hynix, or Samsung memory-segment exposure.
insights.quote: the enterprise-class memory makers of the world ... to do something that they have not been able in five decades: Consistently and predictable make money without a boom/bust cycle.

insights.claim: The author explicitly conditions the thesis: as long as the GenAI boom persists, the memory boom/bust cycle is dead, but if the GenAI bubble bursts, pricing 'comes crashing back down to reality.'
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: This is the key risk flag on the whole thesis: memory-maker profitability is fully coupled to continued AI capex growth, not a genuinely independent structural change — investors should treat any GenAI demand deceleration as a direct downside catalyst for DRAM/HBM pricing and memory-maker earnings.
insights.quote: As long as this GenAI boom persists, the boom/bust cycles that have plagued the for memory and flash markets are dead. If the GenAI bubble bursts, it all comes crashing back down to reality.
insights.locator: In its fiscal third quarter ended in May

insights.claim: Memory chip foundry capacity for both DRAM and NAND is growing only about 20-25% per year, structurally incapable of matching AI-driven demand growth, which the author says guarantees continued price increases regardless of capacity investment.
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: Capacity constraints are physical/capital-cycle-bound (foundry buildout takes years), so memory scarcity pricing is likely to persist for multiple years even if AI demand growth merely stays flat rather than accelerating further.
insights.quote: with bits shipped capacity growing at maybe 20 percent to 25 percent for both DRAM and NAND, most of that increase is a shift from NAND to DRAM and from DRAM to HBM coupled with price increases across the board.
insights.locator: In that May quarter, Micron raked in

insights.claim: Micron has intentionally chosen not to presell all its capacity via long-term contracts, since its five-year agreements with its sixteen largest customers cover only 20% of DRAM capacity and 33% of flash capacity for 2026-2030, leaving the bulk of supply exposed to rising spot/allocation pricing.
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: Micron is deliberately maximizing exposure to rising prices rather than locking in revenue certainty, a strong signal that management itself expects prices to keep climbing — a bullish internal confirmation of the pricing supercycle thesis from the supplier with the most information.
insights.quote: that backlog only represents 20 percent of DRAM capacity and 33 percent of flash capacity between 2026 and 2030 inclusive. The rest of the capacity is subject to ever-increasing prices based on demand.
insights.locator: And while Micron is making a big deal

insights.claim: Micron's five-year agreements with its sixteen largest customers give it a revenue backlog exceeding $100 billion, including roughly $22 billion in upfront cash payments.
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: A $100 billion multi-year backlog with $22 billion of upfront cash materially de-risks Micron's near-term revenue visibility and funds capacity expansion without heavy new debt or equity issuance, a favorable capital-structure signal for the stock.
insights.quote: Micron is making a big deal about having inked five year agreements with enterprises for DRAM and flash with its sixteen largest customers, giving it a revenue backlog of over $100 billion and about getting $22 billion in cash payments upfront
insights.locator: And while Micron is making a big deal

insights.claim: Micron's fiscal Q3 2026 (ended May) revenue hit $41.46 billion, up 4.5X year-on-year and up 73.7% sequentially from Q2 F2026; operating income rose 15.4X to $33.32 billion and net income rose 14X to $28.24 billion.
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: Operating income growing faster (15.4X) than revenue (4.5X) confirms massive operating leverage/margin expansion, not just volume growth — indicates Micron is capturing outsized pricing power rather than just riding higher shipment volumes.
insights.quote: Micron raked in a very impressive $41.46 billion, up by a factor of 4.5X from the year ago period ... Operating income was up a staggering 15.4X to 33.32 billion, and net income rose by a mind-numbing 14X to $28.24 billion.
insights.locator: In that May quarter, Micron raked in

insights.claim: Micron spent only $7.1 billion in capex in the quarter (far below the pace of its revenue/profit growth) and still ended the quarter with $30.1 billion in cash and equivalents, indicating deliberate capital discipline rather than aggressive capacity expansion.
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: Micron (like Samsung and SK Hynix, per the author) is prioritizing price over volume by not racing to add capacity, which sustains the pricing supercycle — a rational oligopoly behavior investors should expect to continue as long as all three majors coordinate on capacity discipline.
insights.quote: capex is not even close to growing at the pace of revenues and profits ... It is far better to push prices higher, particularly when Samsung and SK Hynix are doing the same thing.
insights.locator: Micron spent $7.1 billion in capital expenses

insights.claim: Within Q3 F2026, Micron's DRAM sales were $31.33 billion (up 4.3X year-on-year, up 66.9% sequentially), NAND flash sales were $9.94 billion (with enterprise-class flash slightly over $5 billion of that), and NOR flash sales were $185 million (up 2.5X year-on-year).
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: DRAM is growing faster than NAND within Micron's mix, consistent with the author's broader point that capacity is being reallocated from NAND toward DRAM and HBM — a segment-level breakdown useful for modeling Micron's product-mix margin trajectory.
insights.quote: Micron posted DRAM sales of $31.33 billion, up by a factor of 4.3X compared to the year ago period and up 66.9 percent sequentially. NAND flash storage drove $9.94 billion in sales
insights.locator: In the quarter, Micron posted DRAM sales

insights.claim: Micron's Cloud Memory unit (HBM plus low-power LPDDR memory) posted $13.77 billion in sales (up 4X year-on-year) with $10.74 billion operating income (up 5.9X); its Core Datacenter Group (enterprise flash plus server DRAM) posted $11.52 billion in sales (up 7.5X year-on-year) with $9.57 billion operating income, up 31.3X year-on-year.
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: A 31.3X year-on-year operating income increase in the Core Datacenter Group is an extraordinary margin expansion figure that signals opportunistic pricing power in conventional server DRAM/flash, not just the HBM story — the 'boring' server memory business is now also a high-margin profit engine.
insights.quote: The Core Datacenter Group, which sells enterprise flash as well as normal server DRAM, had sales of $11.52 billion, up 7.5X year on year with operating income of $9.57 billion, up 31.3X year on year. (That is not a typo).
insights.locator: The Cloud Memory unit, which sells HBM

insights.claim: The author estimates (since Micron no longer breaks out HBM separately) that Micron's HBM revenue in Q3 F2026 was about $11.98 billion (up 7.1X year-on-year, up 81.9% sequentially), high-capacity server memory drove $6.48 billion (up 5.4X), and combined 'juicy memory' (HBM + high-capacity server DRAM + LPDDR) totaled $18.46 billion, up 6.4X year-on-year.
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: This is the author's own reconstructed HBM figure (not Micron-disclosed), useful as a comparability benchmark against Samsung's and SK Hynix's disclosed HBM revenues to gauge Micron's relative HBM market share gains.
insights.quote: My model pegs Micron’s HBM revenues in Q3 F2026 at $11.98 billion, up 7.1X year on year and up 81.9 percent sequentially.
insights.locator: Micron no longer talks about HBM sales

insights.claim: Micron's own HBM total addressable market (TAM) forecast for 2026-2030 has grown 2.1X in the last year and a half, from roughly $343 billion (as of December 2024) to roughly $724 billion now, an upward revision the author attributes mostly to price increases rather than capacity growth.
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: A management-disclosed TAM more than doubling in 18 months is a strong forward-looking data point for anyone modeling HBM supplier revenue (Micron, SK Hynix, Samsung) and downstream AI accelerator cost structures (Nvidia, AMD, custom ASIC makers all depend on HBM supply/pricing).
insights.quote: In December 2024, Micron was expecting that aggregate TAM to be around $343 billion ... Now, it looks like it will be around $724 billion.
insights.locator: Micron updated its HBM TAM forecast

insights.claim: Micron guided Q4 F2026 revenue to approximately $50 billion (plus or minus $1 billion), with profits expected to grow faster than revenue.
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: A guided sequential jump from $41.46 billion to ~$50 billion (about 20% quarter-over-quarter) with margin expansion continuing is a concrete near-term forecast investors can check Micron's actual Q4 print against.
insights.quote: Looking ahead to Q4 F2026, Micron expects for revenues to be on the order of $50 billion, give or take $1 billion. Profits will no doubt grow faster than revenues.

themes.theme: HBM lock-in and AI hardware demand durability

insights.claim: The author argues that even if AI compute shifts away from GPUs toward various custom XPUs, demand will not shift away from HBM stacked DRAM memory, because the bandwidth requirement is fundamental and HBM's roadmap is limited by physics and economics, not just current accelerator architecture choices.
insights.speaker: Timothy Prickett Morgan
insights.so_what: This decouples the HBM demand thesis from any single AI accelerator vendor's success (Nvidia, AMD, or custom silicon) — HBM suppliers benefit from the AI buildout broadly regardless of which compute architecture wins, making memory makers a lower-idiosyncratic-risk way to play AI infrastructure growth than any single chip vendor.
insights.quote: Even if people shift their AI engines from GPUs to various XPUs, they are not going to shift away from HBM stacked DRAM memory. The need for bandwidth is too great.
insights.locator: In its fiscal third quarter ended in May
